industries.name: Oil & Gas Drilling Fluids
uses: Barite (barium sulfate, BaSO₄) is ground and added to water-based and oil-based drilling muds as a weighting agent — barite's high density (4.5 g/cm³) and chemical inertness in brine make it the universal choice for increasing mud density to control wellbore pressure
API-grade barite used in drilling fluids must meet specifications for density, particle size, and solubility — API Spec 13A requires ≥4.20 g/cm³ density and specific chemical limits

Drilling mud density is adjusted from ~1.0 to >2.4 g/cm³ with barite loading to balance formation pressure and prevent blowouts — offshore deepwater wells with high pore pressures require very high barite loadings
Barite is consumed (not recovered) during drilling — each well may use hundreds to thousands of tonnes of barite depending on depth and complexity
World barite production exceeds 9 million tonnes per year, the vast majority going to oilfield use — China, India, Morocco, and the US are the largest producers

companies.name: Halliburton
companies.use: Major drilling fluid service company and barite consumer. Formulates and supplies weighted drilling muds containing barite to oil and gas operators globally through its Baroid drilling fluids division.

companies.name: Schlumberger (SLB)
companies.use: World's largest oilfield services company. Supplies barite-weighted drilling fluids through its M-I SWACO drilling fluids division to exploration and production operators in every major oil and gas basin globally.

companies.name: Excalibar Minerals (Newpark Resources)
companies.use: Major barite mining, grinding, and distribution company supplying API-grade barite to oilfield drilling fluid manufacturers. Operates barite grinding facilities in Nevada, Louisiana, and internationally.

industries.name: Medical Imaging (Barium Contrast)
uses: Barium sulfate suspension as oral and rectal X-ray contrast agent for gastrointestinal imaging — barium's high atomic number (Z=56) provides strong X-ray opacity to outline the esophagus, stomach, small bowel, and colon
Upper GI series and barium swallow studies: patient drinks barium sulfate suspension while X-rays capture swallowing mechanics, esophageal structure, and gastric anatomy

Barium enema for colon imaging: double-contrast technique with barium and air outlines the mucosal surface of the large intestine for polyp and cancer detection — largely replaced by CT colonography and colonoscopy but still used globally
Barium sulfate is chemically inert and completely insoluble — passes through GI tract without absorption, making it safe despite barium ion's high toxicity (only the sulfate form is safe)

companies.name: Bracco Imaging
companies.use: Major medical contrast agent producer. Manufactures barium sulfate suspensions (Baroflavine, E-Z-HD) for upper and lower GI fluoroscopic imaging. Also produces iodinated contrast agents for CT and MRI contrast agents.

companies.name: E-Z-EM (Bracco)
companies.share: ~30% of North American barium contrast market
companies.use: Major US barium sulfate contrast agent manufacturer producing a full range of barium preparations for GI imaging procedures at hospitals and radiology centers.

industries.name: Industrial Chemicals & Glass
uses: Barium carbonate (BaCO₃) in specialty glass manufacturing: barium oxide increases refractive index of optical glass for camera and telescope lenses; also used in CRT glass faceplate to absorb X-rays

Barium titanate (BaTiO₃) as a ferroelectric ceramic: the dielectric material in multilayer ceramic capacitors (MLCCs), piezoelectric transducers, and positive temperature coefficient (PTC) thermistors — BaTiO₃ is one of the most important functional ceramic materials
Barium carbonate as rat poison (rodenticide) — barium ion is highly toxic when ingested as soluble salts; use declining due to secondary poisoning risk to predators
Barium peroxide (BaO₂) as initiator in pyrotechnic compositions and as bleaching agent for textiles
Barium chromate as corrosion-inhibiting primer pigment for aircraft aluminum — being phased out due to hexavalent chromium toxicity under REACH regulations

companies.name: Murata Manufacturing
companies.use: World's largest MLCC manufacturer and major consumer of barium titanate dielectric ceramic. Each MLCC contains BaTiO₃ ceramic layers 1–3 microns thick; Murata produces hundreds of billions of MLCCs annually, each containing barium.

companies.name: Sakai Chemical Industry
companies.use: Major barium titanate powder producer supplying MLCC manufacturers worldwide. Produces fine BaTiO₃ powder with precise stoichiometry, particle size, and dopant control required for advanced MLCC dielectric layers.
